mnemonics.title: CFG Components
mnemonics.content: V-T-P-S: Very Tough Problems Surround (V=Variables, T=Terminals, P=Productions, S=Start)

mnemonics.title: CNF Rule
mnemonics.content: CNF = Can't have Non-terminals (only binary or terminal)

mnemonics.title: GNF Rule
mnemonics.content: GNF = Go Nice and Fast (Grammar Needs terminal First)

mnemonics.title: Ambiguity Detection
mnemonics.content: A Grammar is Ambiguous if Multiple Parse trees exist (AMP)

mnemonics.title: Pumping Lemma Parts
mnemonics.content: uvxyz: Under Very eXtra Years (5 parts)

acronyms.usage: Standard form of CFG with A→BC or A→a productions
CFG form where RHS starts with terminal
Language generated by CFG, recognized by PDA
Automaton with stack that recognizes CFLs

visualTips.visualization: Draw as upside-down tree: root at top (start symbol S), branches going down, leaves at bottom are terminals. Each internal node is a non-terminal, each production creates children.
Think of replacing 'cards' in a deck: non-terminals are face-down cards, reveal (replace) them one by one until all are face-up (terminals).
Visualize a vertical spring (stack): push symbols down, pop them up. For a^n b^n, push 'a' for each a, then pop one 'b' for each 'a' - matching happens on stack.
A single string has multiple 'forking paths' - imagine a maze where one entrance leads to multiple exit routes. Same string, different parse tree structures.
Think of it as 'compression': convert long chains like A→BCD into binary tree A→BX, X→CD. Always pair two at a time.

analogies.analogy: CFG is like a recipe: Terminals are ingredients (raw materials), Non-terminals are dishes/sub-recipes, Productions are cooking instructions, Start symbol is the main dish you're making.

Derivation is like assembly IKEA furniture: Start symbol is the boxed set, each production rule tells you which smaller parts connect to make larger parts, final assembled product is the string of terminals.
Parse tree is like a family genealogy: Start symbol S is the ancestor, each generation (level) comes from applying a production, leaves are the final descendants (terminals).
Ambiguous grammar is like a photograph that can be interpreted two ways (like Rubin's vase - faces or vase). Same image (string), two meanings (parse trees).

Regular languages can only count up to a fixed number (like a person with limited memory), CFLs can count arbitrarily (like a person with an infinite notebook/stack). This is why {a^n b^n} needs a stack.
